title: MP Evas and Rape
wikitext:
[]: MP Eva's and Rape             Quote Post by SawItAtAge10 » Sun Nov 09, 2014 5:21 pm I have a theory about the MP Eva's and their heads with "luscious lips" and no eyes. I think they are a literal representation of penis dentada. I say this because I think their viciously brutal attack on Unit 02 (and Asuka for that matter) represent the base carnality of otaku fan boys lusting after the "hot action girl' archetype present in anime. Because these types are generally overtly sexualized, using the MP Eva's as an attack on Asuka during her triumphant sense of total empowerment during EoE represents the lustful masculine reaction to just throwing that type of character in audiences' faces (with particular regard to obsessive and lustful fan boys, think "male gaze"). In that sense, Asuka's "empowerment" could be seen as her rise to fulfilling her absolute sense of femininity. However, as the music playing during that scene ("False Regeneration") suggests, this rise to power is based on a falsehood. Thus, the artifice that she relied to get to that point is turned on her, first with the "penetration" of the fake LoL and then by the actual "pack of wolves feasting upon a sacrificial lamb" type of assault that symbolically represents the rape of her character type. Furthermore, it's interesting that early on Asuka completely disses Eva Units 00 and 01 claiming hers to be "the final production model". Then, the MASS PRODUCED Eva's come along to disprove her pompous theory of Unit 02 being the best thing in the Eva-verse since sliced bread. Now, I'm not bashing Asuka. Like most of the characters in NGE, I like her. I think she's just as complex as most everyone else in the series. However, being a "human" character, she has flaws. One of her major ones is pride. granted her hubris stems from her deeply psychological problems (needing attention, wanting to be loved/approval from others, etc.) I just think that Anno (in his facetiously meta way of storytelling) manages to accomplish a variety of things with this scene: 1. The attack of lustful otaku who take anime far too seriously to the point of pursing it instead of real human interaction. In this case, it's the sexual activity (such as masturbating to deformed hentai versions of characters from various series, including NGE). He uses the MP Eva's to convey them as ravenous vultures. (This 1st level assault from Anno is also linked to the masturbation scene in the hospital) 2. He simultaneously uses this scene to destroy his version of the object of lust that caused the overt sexualization in the first place: Asuka as the "action girl" (in contrast to the "mother" archetype). 3. Provides his own reaction to some people's negative reaction to NGE not being "manly" enough. This "masculine" based attack is completely forced and mechanized. I say this due to the fact that MPEs are driven by Dummy Plugs of Kaworu. Thus, the sexual lusting happens autonomously without thought, implying that this level of thinking comes as a result of "programing" stemming from a greater scale of animation fandom (as in the way so many anime has pushed "hot" women with "giggle physics" without understanding the consequences of doing so) and some general sociological standards regarding sexuality. But also, this would be the most "hetero-normative" that Kaworu has ever acted, and it's done completely on a mechanical basis. Thus, Anno's "pandering" to the Eva audience by saying "Oh, you don't want 'gay' characters and you want them to be 'manly' and to act accordingly Well, here you go!" [Insert Asuka's demise at the hands of the MPEs] Thus, not only does this "further" the plot in EoE, it also allows Anno to make various types of subversive commentaries/deconstruction on his audience and the genre as a whole.

[]:             Quote Post by Rosenakahara » Sun Nov 09, 2014 5:55 pm ^ At this point that isn't really a theory, this has been discussed several times over and i think at this point everyone here realizes that is the way the scene was meant to be interpreted.

[]:             Quote Post by Jäeger » Wed Nov 12, 2014 5:27 am Not a theory : A FACT [url]http://s256.photobucket.com/user/bdbull/media/Feast1.jpg.html[/url]

[]:             Quote Post by Nuclear Lunchbox » Wed Nov 12, 2014 2:47 pm Every time I watch freeze-frames of EoE I swear to whatever that I learn something new and creepier each time. Blech.

[]:             Quote Post by LordThaeon » Thu Nov 13, 2014 2:31 am Jäeger wrote: Not a theory : A FACT [url]http://s256.photobucket.com/user/bdbull/media/Feast1.jpg.html[/url]

[]:             Quote Post by Nuclear Lunchbox » Thu Nov 13, 2014 2:55 am It's not so much flipping the bird as it is adding icing to the cake, I think. You see a cake, and you think it looks good, and you eat a piece of it, and you go, "Man, that's a really good cake." But then somebody puts icing on it, and you eat a piece of that cake, and you go, "Holy shit, this is an even better cake." This is the icing, and it's what makes the people who love EoE go, "This is incredible."

[]:             Quote Post by Monk Ed » Thu Nov 13, 2014 3:14 am LordThaeon wrote: But what's the point? What does the middle finger add that wasn't already present in Asuka's horrifying death?

[]:             Quote Post by Coldwire » Fri Nov 14, 2014 12:04 am Well, the rape undertones are definitely there, but I think it is too far fetched to say this is another middle finger to the otaku, I mean, the hospital scene already does that on a very direct way, why do it again more subtly? I do find interesting the fact that it is Kaworu (albeit, indirectly) the one that massacres Asuka after he also replaces her on episode 24. Well, I guess Kaworu 2, Asuka 0.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Fri Nov 14, 2014 12:24 am The MPE scene is more about rape culture generally than the otaku message. It works on multiple layers and one of those is a critique of the 'strong female character' as the subject of male bondage and domination fantasies that go beyond the bidimensional lusts of the Japanese shut in.

[]:             Quote Post by ElMariachi » Fri Nov 14, 2014 3:45 am ^ What, no mention of the Sun Penis-Gods punishing the female character for attracting the sexual desire of the main character, under the secret desire of EVA-01/Yui the Monster Mother to keep Shinji forever in a dependent and sexless state in her metaphorical womb? I'm disappointed Chuckman.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Fri Nov 14, 2014 12:29 pm Jäeger wrote: Not a theory : A FACT [url]http://s256.photobucket.com/user/bdbull/media/Feast1.jpg.html[/url]

[]:             Quote Post by Jäeger » Fri Nov 14, 2014 4:15 pm Reichu wrote: That doesn't really prove anything in and of itself, though I guess it's "proof" if you've already decided what the scene means. Now, the item to post if you're going to announce that the rape subtext is a "fact" is the snippet from the available draft of episode 26' where it's specified that the scene should evoke rape. Although, that same specification is missing from the final screenplay...

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Fri Nov 14, 2014 4:20 pm I said there are multiple layers. Those are some of the others. There's a lot going on. Ever set piece in EoE is like that.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Fri Nov 14, 2014 4:45 pm Jäeger wrote: I'm pretty sure most people who see that frame evoke rape.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Fri Nov 14, 2014 5:05 pm Honestly I don’t see much of EoE as having a “take that, otaku” vibe to it. Even the moment where Anno cuts to various forms of audience feedback had both negative vandalism and positive letters included. To say that the whole film or even parts of the film was Anno flipping off the fans would include the fans Anno recognized supporting his artistic endeavor and not just to the ones that lashed out against him. Asuka was probably going to die all along. I’m not one to promote congruency theory, but we do see Asuka in Unit 02 hiding in the lake back in EoTV. This suggests to me that Asuka dying probably wasn’t anything new in the minds of the filmmakers, and certainly wasn’t a knee-jerk reaction to negative fan feedback that EoTV cause. Back in the context of the work itself, Asuka is portrayed as a very sexually wanting character. There are freudian reasons for that, of course, but I think we tend to miss how others react to her sexual desires. Kaji outright rejects her, Shinji is confused by her, Rei is seen as competition, and finally Asuka attempts suicide in Ep. 23. She’s found naked in a bath tub and hospitalized. After which she finally starts receiving some sexual attention. Shinji masturbates over her body (which she realizes but can’t respond to), and then later on the MPAs finally get to her in a rather grotesque and fashion that’s evocative of sexual perversion. If anything, in that context, it shows that none of the characters really get what they want this side of Instrumentality, including Asuka. Misato ultimately can’t face her father complexes, not even through Kaji, Rei can’t obey Gendo like she was once able to, Shinji can’t find where his happiness lies, and Asuka will never establish herself as a mature sexual being. Instead these goals get crushed or become completely subverted. Misato gives up the memorial of her father to Shinji after Kaji’s death, Rei obeys another Ikari, Shinji gets what he wished for but finds it alienating, and Asuka will never actually have a consensual relationship. Whether or not they find these fulfillments through Instrumentality is another topic for another thread.

[]:             Quote Post by SawItAtAge10 » Fri Nov 14, 2014 5:15 pm First of all, I'd like to thank everyone for their feedback on this topic. I appreciate all of you for providing some of your thoughts on this theory. One other thing I'd like to add is how Asuka (as a sexualized action girl) is a kind of "Cool Girl". For those who don't know, this is a term coined by the author Gillian Flynn. Here concept is that the "Cool Girl" represents the ideal of womanhood from a man's (as in a "masculine" man who objectifies women) perspective in that a female counterpart will always try to be whatever she needs to be in order to live up to man's expectations. This can take on many forms: the aforementioned "sexy action girl" (Asuka) "girl next door type, typically very "motherly" (much like Rei, albeit in a very creepy Oedipal kind of way. In certain respects, this is actually closer to Hikari, in that she's just an "ordinary girl") "manic pixie dream girl" (which doesn't seem to exist in NGE, unless one considers Misato with her overtly chatty nature and character position of existing solely to inspire Shinji to keep carrying on. Incidentally, she also often shifts between/embodies the other two- as both "mother type", and "sexualized action type") My point here is that Flynn's concepts are applicable in that they don't represent fully functional/complex human beings as much as they are mere constructs of a person (very 2 dimensional, much like animation itself, ha ha) Thus, the only way Asuka can become a "real girl" is to be stripped of her "badassery" in the most vulgar and subtle ways. Vulgar: The masturbation, violent defeat at the hands of the MPEs Subtle: The loss of her position as an Eva pilot, whether the series ended on ideal conditions with the conflict simply being over with the angels destroyed or in the horrifying manner seen in EoE. Either way, her status as action heroine would no longer apply. I might add that Flynn's take on the artificalized concepts of women are deeply feminist in nature, which 'the strength of women" is often sexualized irregardless what route the creator of a work takes. It's as though the "male gaze" is a kind of curse that will infect and ultimately undermine any kind of empowerment for females. Thus, it's better to figuratively and literally destroy their representations (as seen with the demise of Rei III/Lilith, Asuka, and Misato in EoE) than to let this kind of sick charade continue on. Now, I suppose that "beach Asuka" is a more finalized version of herself in that she has surpassed the need to get validation by being sexual or being an "action chick", but the details on how she got their a kind of sketchy since both the NGE ending and EoE take the route of "there's far too little time, so let's just focus on Shinji".

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Sat Nov 22, 2014 5:09 am SawItAtAge10 wrote: I might add that Flynn's take on the artificalized concepts of women are deeply feminist in nature, which 'the strength of women" is often sexualized irregardless what route the creator of a work takes. It's as though the "male gaze" is a kind of curse that will infect and ultimately undermine any kind of empowerment for females. Thus, it's better to figuratively and literally destroy their representations (as seen with the demise of Rei III/Lilith, Asuka, and Misato in EoE) than to let this kind of sick charade continue on.

[]:             Quote Post by Tumbling Down » Tue Dec 02, 2014 5:29 pm The idea that anything in that scene had a rape connotation never even crossed my mind. Maybe it's because I'm not into that sort o-- Chuckman wrote: The MPE scene is more about rape culture generally than the otaku message. It works on multiple layers and one of those is a critique of the 'strong female character' as the subject of male bondage and domination fantasies that go beyond the bidimensional lusts of the Japanese shut in.
